concepts_addressed: Contrarian Conviction as Outsized Return Driver

frameworks.name: Galapagos Islands Loyalty Framework
frameworks.definition: A loyalty program treated as a behavioral observation environment: combining longitudinal individual-level data with a psychologist's mindset to surface anomalies, paradoxes, and small-effect insights that reveal how humans actually decide, as distinct from how economists assume they decide.

frameworks.name: Explore-Exploit Trade-off (Bee Waggle Dance Model)
frameworks.definition: A resource-allocation framework in which roughly 20% of effort is devoted to random exploration of unknown opportunities (ignoring the waggle dance) and 80% exploits known reliable sources; pure exploitation produces local-maximum traps and eventual extinction.

frameworks.name: SCARF Model (David Rock)
frameworks.definition: Five dimensions humans deeply care about that economic data never captures: Status, Certainty, Autonomy, Relatedness, Fairness; marketing and loyalty programs that address these create engagement that price discounts cannot replicate.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that for any business or marketing problem, the logical solution is safe but mediocre, while a psychologically grounded alternative — however strange — can be dramatically more effective. The competitive advantage in marketing lies specifically in those non-obvious, behavioral solutions because they are systematically under-explored: it is much easier to be fired for being illogical than for being unimaginative, so organizations self-select toward boring-but-defensible answers.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: organizations dominated by finance or engineering culture
problems involving human behavior, not pure engineering
claims.mechanism: Career risk is asymmetric: illogical decisions get punished while unimaginative ones do not, so rational employees systematically avoid creative solutions even when those solutions would be superior.
bounded_by: Pure engineering or mathematical problems where logical reasoning is a good proxy for outcome quality

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that data only tells you what is happening, not why — and that without the why, data cannot guide action. Behavioral science is necessary to supply the causal interpretation; a loyalty program without behavioral science leaves the marketer no better off than they were before collecting data.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: complex systems involving human behavior
claims.mechanism: Data is collected and aggregated for upward reporting (shareholders want totals); this aggregation strips out the path-dependent, longitudinal, individual-level variation that reveals causal mechanisms.
bounded_by: Simple or complicated engineering systems where there is a single right answer

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that the average customer, as computed from aggregate transaction data, is either nonexistent or wildly unrepresentative. In psychology, very small stimuli produce very large effects — a property true in complex systems but not in simple ones — so the most valuable data points are exceptions, anomalies, and paradoxes, not averages.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: complex adaptive systems (markets, human behavior)
not applicable to simple or purely mechanical systems
claims.mechanism: Small things have big effects in complex systems because of feedback loops, nonlinearity, and path dependence; averaging across customers destroys the very variation that reveals these effects.
bounded_by: Physics, rocketry, and other simple deterministic systems where effect sizes are proportional to cause sizes

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that cutting prices is the worst possible way to give customers money because it (a) sets a permanently lower expectation for future pricing, (b) contains the signal of desperation, and (c) is margin-eroding without creating the emotional excitement that an equivalent-value loyalty mechanism or gamified incentive would generate. He argues that every alternative should be exhausted before a price cut is used.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: consumer-facing businesses with repeat-purchase opportunity
claims.mechanism: A price cut reduces absolute cost to all customers uniformly — a one-time signal that fades quickly from memory — whereas gamified or loyalty-based equivalents create ongoing emotional engagement, FOMO, and social conversation at the same or lower actual cost.
bounded_by: Commoditized markets with no brand differentiation where price may be the only lever
One-time transactions with no repeat-purchase relationship

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that consumers value '50% extra free' on a shampoo bottle much more than a '33% off' price reduction on the same bottle, even though the two offers are mathematically identical in unit cost to the consumer. Critically, the '50% extra free' mechanic is also less margin-eroding for the brand.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: packaged consumer goods with physical volume (e.g., shampoo, food)
claims.mechanism: Consumers use the product's visible physical quantity as the perceived-value frame, not the unit-price calculation; 'more product' triggers a different mental anchor than 'lower price'.

numbers.axis: perceived consumer value of equivalent discount mechanics

bounded_by: Digital or intangible products where no physical volume signal exists

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that MCI's 'Friends and Family' calling plan (introduced in the early 1990s) generated an order of magnitude more customer engagement than an equivalent flat price reduction would have, despite the discount being economically modest — roughly 20% off calls to a named list of 10-15 people. The program created social conversation, pub debate, and comedic cultural penetration for about eight years; an equivalent price cut would have been forgotten in three months.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: telecommunications or subscription services with recurring purchases
programs that create social-network effects between customers
claims.mechanism: A named, personalized discount creates an identity and social dimension — 'who is in your circle?' — that generates ongoing conversation and FOMO, whereas a uniform price reduction is processed as a one-time financial event and quickly forgotten.

numbers.axis: duration of customer engagement with equivalent discount

anchors.engagement_duration: approximately 8 years of active cultural salience

bounded_by: Products with no social or network dimension (e.g., industrial commodities)

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that tax rebates are psychologically far more effective than equivalent tax rate cuts at generating sustained taxpayer gratitude, conversation, and political goodwill. A rebate check received annually is talked about, planned around, and emotionally salient for months; a rate reduction from 25% to 22% produces a brief feeling of gratitude that fades within eight months. In economic terms the two are identical; in human emotional terms they are completely different.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: government tax policy contexts
any recurring-payment relationship where the payer experiences the 'gift' as a distinct event
claims.mechanism: A lump-sum rebate is experienced as a discrete, positively-valenced event that triggers anticipation, planning, and social discussion; a rate reduction is absorbed into background noise and loses its psychological distinctiveness almost immediately.

numbers.axis: sustained psychological salience of equivalent tax benefit

anchors.salience_duration: ~4 months of active planning and conversation per year

bounded_by: One-time transactions with no ongoing relationship

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that points in loyalty programs are often more valuable to consumers than cash of equivalent monetary value because points enable guilt-free indulgence. Cash spent on luxuries triggers feelings of shame and financial guilt; points, which cannot be used to pay essential bills, do not arouse the same guilt. This effect is especially pronounced during recessions.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: consumer loyalty programs with accumulated points
particularly salient during economic downturns or recession
claims.mechanism: Points are mentally ring-fenced from household budget calculations because they cannot be used for necessities (gas bills, electricity); this removes the opportunity-cost guilt that spending cash on luxuries triggers.
bounded_by: Cash-back programs where points convert directly to statement credits usable on necessities
High-net-worth customers for whom cash-guilt is negligible

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that about 70% of Boots Advantage Card holders (a UK loyalty program) are women, and a common behavioral pattern is that when a female cardholder sends a partner to redeem points for pharmacy items, the partner redeems accumulated points for mundane necessities rather than the aspirational rewards the cardholder had been saving toward. This creates strong negative emotion precisely because the points were being mentally reserved for a guilt-free luxury purchase.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: loyalty programs where points accumulate toward aspirational rewards
household contexts where cards are shared between partners with different mental accounting
claims.mechanism: Partners who are not the primary cardholder lack knowledge of the cardholder's mental reservation of those points for a specific future reward; uncoordinated redemption destroys the planned guilt-free indulgence.

numbers.axis: share of Boots Advantage Card holders by gender

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that when Selfridges (the UK department store) introduced their 'Selfridges Plus' subscription membership, the average number of annual purchases per customer from their website increased from one to five — a 5x lift in purchase frequency from a single program change.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: premium retail subscription membership programs

numbers.axis: average annual purchases per Selfridges website customer

bounded_by: Rory Sutherland did not provide independent verification of this figure; the claim is attributed to Selfridges program data

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that Amazon Prime's model — a paid subscription that creates strong frequency incentives — is dramatically under-copied by retailers, and that frequent customers of any subscription program feel they are 'gaming the system' (saving money on every purchase) which creates a powerful ongoing positive emotional relationship that transactional discounts cannot replicate.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: businesses with high repeat-purchase potential
categories where shipping or access costs accumulate with frequency
claims.mechanism: Prepaid membership converts the customer's mental accounting frame: every subsequent purchase feels like a 'saving' relative to the subscription fee already paid, creating a persistent positive-affect signal absent from per-transaction pricing.
bounded_by: One-purchase or very-infrequent purchase categories where frequency benefit is negligible

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that the Starbucks stored-value card exploits a mental accounting phenomenon: when a customer preloads $30 onto the card and spends $20 on coffee, it feels like a $10 expenditure rather than a $20 expenditure, because the customer has already mentally committed the full $30 to Starbucks. Worldwide, approximately $1 billion sits unused on Starbucks stored-value cards at any one time, demonstrating the financial value of this mechanism to the business.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: prepaid stored-value card programs
high-frequency consumer purchases (coffee, food)
claims.mechanism: Pre-commitment of funds to a single merchant transfers the saliency of cost from the point of purchase to the point of loading; at purchase time, the consumer perceives the transaction cost as only the incremental draw-down on a pre-allocated bucket, not as a fresh outflow of discretionary cash.

numbers.axis: outstanding stored value on Starbucks cards globally

anchors.state: standing balance at any one time

bounded_by: Single-use or low-frequency purchase categories where stored-value loading would not be adopted

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that approximately 45% of airline frequent flyer program members admit to having arranged a completely unnecessary business trip solely to avoid dropping out of their current tier status before the annual reset date. This demonstrates that tier psychology creates behavioral changes of such magnitude that customers will incur real costs — time, money, carbon — to preserve a status level.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: tiered loyalty programs with annual recertification deadlines
business travelers with discretionary control over trip scheduling
claims.mechanism: Status tier expiry operates as a loss-aversion trigger: the pain of dropping a tier is felt more strongly than the equivalent gain of achieving it, motivating irrational effort to prevent loss.

numbers.axis: share of airline frequent flyers who have made a gratuitous trip to defend tier status

bounded_by: Loyalty programs without tier structures or annual recertification

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that loyalty programs are structurally superior to price cuts as a mechanism for creating perceived value because they allow the same economic outlay to generate dramatically more emotional engagement, social conversation, and customer identity investment. He frames this as an insight extractable from loyalty programs that applies across all of marketing: precision discounting (targeted, personalized, gamified) beats uniform discounting on engagement by an order of magnitude.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: consumer businesses with repeat-purchase potential
brands seeking to protect pricing power
claims.mechanism: Uniform price reductions are processed by consumers as a background change to a reference price and quickly habituated; personalized or gamified value delivery creates discrete, emotionally salient events that trigger anticipation, social sharing, and identity investment.
bounded_by: Pure commodity markets with zero brand differentiation where price is the only decision variable

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that the Uber ride-tracking map does not reduce the duration of waiting time but fundamentally transforms its quality by eliminating uncertainty, converting aversive unknown-wait-time into relaxed, plannable known-wait-time. This illustrates a general principle: in service businesses, certainty about wait time can be more valuable than speed itself.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: on-demand service businesses where wait time is unavoidable
claims.mechanism: Uncertainty about an aversive event (waiting) occupies attention continuously and generates anxiety; precise information about the event's endpoint converts the same duration into plannable, psychologically manageable time.
bounded_by: Services where wait time is so short that uncertainty is irrelevant (under 30 seconds)

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that for a flight or long journey, the quality of time — whether it is productive, entertaining, or relaxing — matters as much as or more than the quantity of time to most travelers. Adding Wi-Fi to Eurostar trains would likely produce a comparable or greater improvement in propensity to take the train versus flying than spending £6 billion to reduce journey time by 40 minutes, at roughly 0.001% of the cost.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: inter-city transport competing with alternatives (train vs. plane)
claims.mechanism: Engineers define problems in terms amenable to engineering solutions (speed); the actual behavioral question is 'what makes people choose this mode?' — and quality of time (productive, entertaining) is a more powerful driver than quantity of time saved.

numbers.axis: cost of equivalent improvement in propensity to take Eurostar vs. fly

anchors.intervention: reduce Paris-London journey by ~40 minutes (high-speed rail investment)

bounded_by: Ultra-short journeys (under 30 minutes) where time quality is irrelevant
Business travelers for whom only arrival time matters contractually

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that many products and behaviors that consumers say they do not want in advance become indispensable once experienced — a ratchet effect where a single exposure permanently resets preferences. He cites Japanese toilets, mobile phones, multi-channel television, electric cars, Nespresso capsules, and Red Bull as examples where there was zero pre-existing consumer demand and, in several cases, active research-based rejection, yet post-adoption stickiness was near-total.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: novel product categories where consumers have no experiential reference point
products that change the consumer's utility function upon first use
claims.mechanism: Pre-adoption research measures preferences of the pre-adoption self; the product, when experienced, changes what the person values and wants. Asking the pre-adoption self whether they want the product is therefore a structurally misleading research methodology.
bounded_by: Incremental product improvements within existing categories where consumers can reliably evaluate in advance
Products with immediate visible costs and no latent experiential transformation

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that behavioral change follows a sigmoid (S-curve) pattern: slow initial uptake due to the gravitational forces of habit and social copying, then rapid acceleration when both habit and social norm shift simultaneously. Many valuable innovations are abandoned prematurely because business expects linear growth and treats the slow initial phase as evidence of failure rather than evidence of normal adoption dynamics.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: novel behavior categories where habit and social norms must shift together
claims.mechanism: Habit formation and social norm adoption are independent but mutually reinforcing forces; until both pass a threshold simultaneously, visible uptake is slow. When both cross threshold together, adoption accelerates non-linearly.
bounded_by: Incremental product improvements in established categories without habitual or social friction

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that without brands and without the expectation of repeat purchase, capitalism degenerates into a race to the bottom in which every manufacturer is incentivized only to meet minimum specifications at minimum cost and to innovate not at all, because there is no mechanism by which a customer can preferentially reward a better product. He cites the Soviet Union's manufacturing record as the historical proof case.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.topic: Contrarian Conviction as Outsized Return Driver
conditions: market economies with repeat-purchase dynamics
claims.mechanism: Brands are the selection units through which end consumers reward quality (via repeat purchase) or punish failure (via defection); without distinct named brands, consumer reward signals cannot be routed to the specific producers who earned them.
bounded_by: Pure commodity markets where product is genuinely homogeneous and brand differentiation is impossible

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that in a business context, the optimal resource allocation between exploration (testing unknown, often-daft ideas) and exploitation (optimizing known successful tactics) is never 100% exploitation. He suggests that approximately 30-40% of a loyalty program's activities should be devoted to exploration precisely because the loyalty program's measurement infrastructure allows affordable, manageable-scale testing — making it the natural home of a company's exploration budget.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: businesses operating in complex, shifting markets with loyalty program infrastructure
claims.mechanism: Pure exploitation eventually traps a business in a local maximum; without exploration, the business cannot grow or respond when its known successful channels degrade. The loyalty program's ability to test hypotheses on small, measurable subsets of customers makes it the most cost-effective exploration vehicle within the enterprise.

numbers.axis: recommended share of loyalty program activity devoted to exploration vs. exploitation

bounded_by: Organizations without the data infrastructure to cheaply test and measure exploration outcomes
Early-stage businesses that have not yet found a single working channel

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that displaying a specific flight-delay duration (e.g., 'Flight 249 delayed 47 minutes') causes far less customer distress than displaying only 'Delayed' with no time estimate. Certainty, even about a negative event, is psychologically preferable to uncertainty. He offers this as evidence that the SCARF dimension of Certainty is a primary driver of customer satisfaction that standard business metrics never capture.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: any service with unavoidable wait times or delays
claims.mechanism: Uncertainty about an aversive future event occupies cognitive attention continuously; a specific endpoint converts open-ended dread into plannable adaptation, dramatically reducing experienced distress.
bounded_by: Cases where the stated duration turns out to be wrong — inaccurate specificity can be worse than honest uncertainty

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that the American Express 'Member Since [year]' card feature is an effectively costless mechanism that dramatically increases customer relationship depth. He cites an example where American Express accidentally issued replacement cards with wrong member-since years, and more than half of affected cardholders contacted American Express to demand the original date be reinstated — demonstrating how deeply customers value tenure recognition.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: financial services and premium card products
subscription or membership businesses with long-tenure customers
claims.mechanism: Human relationships are psychologically path-dependent: the length of a relationship changes the implied obligations and the meaning of any single transaction within it. Acknowledging tenure publicly validates the customer's investment in the relationship.

numbers.axis: share of affected American Express cardholders who contacted AmEx to correct wrong member-since year

anchors.action: contacted AmEx to reinstate correct date

bounded_by: Low-involvement transactional products where customers have no relationship identity investment

claims.text: Rory Sutherland claims that a Dishoom restaurant loyalty mechanic — giving best customers a die to roll during off-peak hours (Monday–Thursday, pay before 6pm), with a one-in-six chance of the entire meal being free — generated dramatically more customer excitement and positive word-of-mouth than an equivalent 16.6% price reduction would have. Wealthy customers who could afford to buy the restaurant many times over were captivated by the gamble, while they would not have crossed the street for a 16.6% discount.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: premium restaurant or hospitality contexts
customers for whom the absolute economic value of the discount is negligible
claims.mechanism: Variable-ratio reinforcement (the chance of winning) is intrinsically more engaging than fixed-ratio reinforcement (guaranteed discount); the social and conversational salience of a potential win far exceeds that of a predictable saving.

numbers.axis: effective discount level vs. engagement generated

anchors.engagement: high — social sharing, group rituals, cross-city word-of-mouth among wealthy PE professionals
negligible — wealthy customers would not cross the street for it

bounded_by: Price-sensitive customers for whom absolute savings dominate psychological effects
Brands without sufficient trust where gambling mechanics might feel exploitative

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that in a call center context, engineering a callback option — where a customer requests a call back rather than waiting on hold — makes cross-selling and upselling an order of magnitude easier than if the customer waited on hold. The mechanism is a psychological shift: waiting makes the customer feel like a supplicant, while being called back makes them feel like a valued customer. He acknowledges he does not have empirical proof of the magnitude but regards this as a testable hypothesis with high prior probability.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: call centers with outbound callback capability
contexts involving upsell or cross-sell during service interactions
claims.mechanism: Status psychology (SCARF: Status dimension) determines receptivity in sales conversations; a customer who waited on hold is in a subordinate, irritated state; a customer who received a proactive callback perceives themselves as valued, lowering defensive resistance.
bounded_by: Speaker explicitly notes he lacks empirical proof; this is a stated hypothesis requiring testing
Callbacks must occur within a promised timeframe or the benefit is reversed

examples.text: UNESCO received a large donation of Crocs shoes from the shoe company. The obvious logical response was to distribute the shoes to people in need. Instead, the head of partnerships gave the shoes to schools, awarding a free pair to every child who attended school in their first week. This created a second-order behavioral effect: children who missed day one heard that attendees received free shoes, and showed up on days two and three to avoid missing out. The same resource delivered both shoe distribution AND a school-attendance habit incentive by deploying non-obvious behavioral logic.

A wealthy private equity firm from Chicago always visited Dishoom in London on their UK trips. They brought a specific colleague they believed had a 'hot hand' for rolling sixes, because Dishoom's loyalty mechanic gave best customers during off-peak hours a die to roll — a six meant the entire meal was free. The group had won three free meals over multiple visits and were enthusiastically engaged with the program. Each of these individuals could have afforded to buy the restaurant many times over — a flat 16.6% discount would not have interested them at all. The gamble created social rituals, group identity, and cross-Atlantic word-of-mouth.

Rory Sutherland, about to give a talk in London, soaked the front of his trousers in a wash basin with a ski-ramp shape. Standing at the hairdryer for several minutes had no effect. Rather than simply canceling, he walked outside — where it had begun to rain — and stood in the downpour until he was entirely soaked. When he appeared on stage, the audience wondered why he had climbed out of a swimming pool, but this was less embarrassing than the alternative inference from a partially-soaked groin. The story illustrates that in complex problems, the counterintuitive opposite of the obvious solution is sometimes the only workable one.

Nespresso capsules created a product category — paying ~$0.70-0.80 per home-made cup of coffee — that had no prior consumer demand and would have been rejected outright if presented as a $30-40 jar of equivalent coffee. The product succeeded entirely by changing the consumer's price reference frame: capsules are compared to a Starbucks coffee (~$3.60), not to a jar of instant coffee. This is a psychological breakthrough achieved entirely through packaging and pricing architecture, not product superiority.

Red Bull was submitted to a market research firm specializing in carbonated beverages before launch. The firm described it as the worst drink ever researched in the company's history: usual feedback on drinks is that they are 'a bit sweet' or 'more for kids'; Red Bull generated responses including 'I wouldn't drink this piss if you paid me to.' Despite unanimous negative pre-launch research, Red Bull became one of the most commercially successful beverage launches in 50 years, illustrating that pre-adoption consumer research systematically fails to predict ratchet products.

A Michelin-starred French restaurant (unnamed, sent to Rory Sutherland) wanted to discourage mobile phone use during meals. The obvious approach was a sign saying 'Mobile phones not allowed.' Instead, the restaurant posted a sign ostensibly targeted at departing guests: 'Please remember to turn your cell phone back on when you leave.' This implicitly established a social norm ('our guests naturally do not use phones during dinner') rather than issuing a prohibition, achieving the same behavior via identity-consistent framing rather than bossing.

Reader's Digest embedded a loyalty mechanic directly into the physical product: the January issue displayed one Pegasus logo on the spine, February two, March three, and so on. If a subscriber let their subscription lapse, the progression was broken and their bookshelf 'looked a bit shit.' The pattern disruption created a tangible aesthetic cost to non-renewal that required no active reminder from the brand. Rory Sutherland describes this as one of the most elegant embedded loyalty mechanics he has seen.

British Airways flight departure boards: displaying 'Flight 249 delayed 47 minutes' causes minimal customer distress; displaying only 'Delayed' with no timeframe causes severe anxiety and complaints. The quantity of delay is the same but the psychological experience is completely different. Rory Sutherland cites this when arguing that certainty (the C in David Rock's SCARF model) is one of the primary drivers of customer experience, yet it is never captured in standard business metrics.

Air fryers were first introduced and evangelized years before mass adoption, with Rory Sutherland describing himself as the 'John the Baptist of air fryers' — preaching their value with little initial traction. Only after TikTok content created both habitual familiarity and social normalization simultaneously did adoption accelerate rapidly. He uses air fryers as the concrete illustration of the sigmoid behavioral-change curve: many ideas that seem to fail in their early phase are actually in the 'slow sigmoid' portion and would succeed if not abandoned prematurely.

Bees have evolved the waggle dance, a mechanism for communicating the direction and distance of reliable nectar sources. Bee scientists discovered that approximately 20% of bees ignore the waggle dance entirely and forage at random. Over 20 million years of evolution, this 20% random exploration has proven essential: pure waggle-dance compliance (100% exploitation) leads to hive extinction when the known source fails, whereas the random explorers discover new fields and share their discoveries with the hive. Rory Sutherland uses this as the empirical foundation for his 30-40% exploration allocation recommendation in loyalty programs.

practices.text: Test small, seemingly trivial changes to call-center opening scripts and customer service phraseology before spending on infrastructure; tweaking tonality alone has generated several million pounds per year in business value in documented cases.
If you run a call center, engineer a callback option so customers wait no more than two minutes on hold; call them back within a couple of hours rather than making them wait.

Before cutting prices, exhaust every alternative: points, gamification, exclusivity, bonus tiers, gifts, or bonus-volume mechanics that can deliver equivalent economic value with far greater emotional salience.

In loyalty programs, allocate a proportion of the test budget to testing ideas that seem genuinely silly or unjustifiable in advance; ratchet-effect innovations — like the Dishoom free-meal die — are not discoverable through pre-rationalized research.
On flight and hotel departure displays, always show a specific time estimate for delays rather than only showing 'Delayed'; certainty reduces experienced distress even when the news is bad.

When evaluating a new business or product, ask the stickiness question: of the people who have done this once, how many do it again? This is the most load-bearing behavioral signal for long-term success, and is rarely asked.
Do not display a banking app user's account balance immediately on login, as most customers prefer not to be confronted with this information by default.

Make discounts exclusive to loyalty program members rather than applying them across the board; humans value relative savings (available only to me) more than absolute savings (available to everyone).
